sections.heading: What is Jetpack Compose?
sections.content: Jetpack Compose is Android's modern, fully declarative UI toolkit, released as stable by Google in 2021. It replaces the traditional View-based XML UI system with a Kotlin-only approach where you describe what the UI should look like for a given state, and Compose handles rendering and updating it automatically. Instead of inflating XML layouts, finding views by ID, and imperatively updating them, you write composable functions that return UI descriptions — and whenever the underlying state changes, Compose efficiently re-executes only the affected composables and updates the screen. Compose is now the recommended approach for building new Android UI.

sections.heading: Why Jetpack Compose?
items: Declarative — describe what the UI looks like for a given state; Compose handles the how
Kotlin-only — no XML, no View inflation, no findViewById; everything is Kotlin code
Less code — typical Compose UI requires significantly fewer lines than equivalent View-based XML
Powerful — custom layouts, animations, and gestures that were complex in Views are straightforward in Compose
Reactive — UI automatically updates when state changes; no manual notifyDataSetChanged() or setText()
Interoperable — Compose works alongside existing View-based code; migrate incrementally
Testable — composables are plain Kotlin functions; easy to unit test and UI test in isolation
Single source of truth — UI logic and UI structure live together in the same composable function
Google recommended — all new Android UI development guidance from Google uses Compose

rows.compose: Kotlin composable functions — UI and logic in one place
rows.view_system: XML layout files inflated at runtime; logic in separate Activity/Fragment

rows.compose: Automatic — Compose recomposes when state changes
rows.view_system: Manual — call setText(), setVisibility(), notifyDataSetChanged() explicitly

rows.compose: None needed — UI is rebuilt from state
rows.view_system: findViewById or View Binding to get references to update

rows.compose: MaterialTheme composable with typed color, typography, and shape tokens
rows.view_system: XML styles, themes, and attribute resolution

rows.compose: LazyColumn / LazyRow — built-in efficient list composables
animate*AsState, AnimatedVisibility, Transition APIs — all Kotlin
Canvas in Compose — DrawScope API with Kotlin DSL
rows.view_system: Override onDraw() in a custom View subclass

rows.compose: ComposeTestRule — semantics-based; no View hierarchy traversal needed
@Preview annotation — instant previews in Android Studio without running the app
rows.view_system: XML layout editor — limited fidelity; requires custom View attributes

sections.heading: Composable Functions
sections.content: The building block of Compose UI is the composable function — an ordinary Kotlin function annotated with @Composable. A composable function describes a piece of UI by calling other composable functions. It does not return a View; instead it emits UI elements into the composition. Composable functions can be called only from other composable functions or from a Compose entry point. They can read state, accept parameters, and contain logic, but they should be side-effect free — their output should depend only on their inputs and the state they observe.

sections.heading: Composable Function Rules and Conventions
items: Annotate with @Composable — required for any function that emits UI or calls other composables
Name with PascalCase — composable functions follow the same naming convention as classes: Greeting(), UserCard()
No return value for UI composables — they emit UI, they don't return View objects
Can accept parameters — pass data down from parent to child composables as function parameters
Can read state — access remembered state or ViewModel StateFlow within composables
Should be idempotent — calling the same composable with the same inputs should produce the same UI
Should be side-effect free — avoid writing to global state, launching coroutines, or performing I/O directly in the composable body
Can contain control flow — if/else and when expressions naturally control which composables are emitted
Composables can call any regular Kotlin function — they are just functions
Only composable functions can call other composable functions — the @Composable annotation is viral

rows.description: Displays a string of text with optional styling
A Material Design button with click handling
Displays an image from a painter resource
An editable text input field
rows.example: TextField(value = text, onValueChange = { text = it }, label = { Text("Name") })

rows.description: Arranges children vertically — equivalent to vertical LinearLayout
Arranges children horizontally — equivalent to horizontal LinearLayout
Stacks children on top of each other — equivalent to FrameLayout
Efficiently renders a vertical scrollable list — equivalent to RecyclerView
An empty composable that takes up space — adds padding/gap between elements
Renders a Material Design vector icon
Implements the Material Design visual layout structure with TopBar, BottomBar, FAB, and content slots
A Material surface with elevation, shape, and color — wraps content in a styled container

sections.heading: The Modifier System
sections.content: In Compose, layout and appearance properties are applied through the Modifier system rather than XML attributes or View properties. A Modifier is a chain of immutable transformations applied to a composable — controlling its size, padding, click behavior, background, border, scroll behavior, and much more. Modifiers are passed as a parameter to virtually every composable, and the order in which you chain modifier operations matters significantly — padding before size has a different result than size before padding.

sections.heading: Essential Modifier Operations
items: Modifier.fillMaxSize() — makes the composable fill its parent's width and height
Modifier.fillMaxWidth() — fills the parent's full width
Modifier.fillMaxHeight() — fills the parent's full height
Modifier.size(dp) — sets exact width and height
Modifier.width(dp) / Modifier.height(dp) — sets width or height independently
Modifier.padding(dp) — adds uniform padding on all sides
Modifier.padding(horizontal = dp, vertical = dp) — sets horizontal and vertical padding separately
Modifier.padding(start = dp, top = dp, end = dp, bottom = dp) — sets each side independently
Modifier.background(color) — sets background color
Modifier.background(color, shape = RoundedCornerShape(8.dp)) — sets background with shape
Modifier.border(width, color, shape) — draws a border around the composable
Modifier.clip(shape) — clips the composable to a shape (circle, rounded rect)
Modifier.clickable { action } — makes the composable respond to click events
Modifier.wrapContentSize() — sizes the composable to wrap its content

rows.result: Click target is the inner area (padding is outside the clickable region)
Click target includes the padding area (padding is inside the clickable region)
Background fills the full size; content is inset by padding
Background only covers the inner area (padding is outside the background)
size(100.dp) wins — fillMaxWidth is constrained by the preceding size

sections.heading: State in Compose
sections.content: State is the engine that drives Compose's reactivity. When state changes, Compose automatically re-executes (recomposes) the composables that read that state. The primary way to hold local UI state inside a composable is with remember { mutableStateOf(initialValue) }. remember preserves the value across recompositions of the same composable. mutableStateOf creates an observable state holder — any composable that reads it will recompose when it changes. For state that must survive configuration changes (screen rotation), use rememberSaveable instead of remember.

sections.heading: State Fundamentals
items: var count by remember { mutableStateOf(0) } — declares local state that triggers recomposition when changed
by keyword — Kotlin property delegation; lets you read/write the state value directly without .value
val state = remember { mutableStateOf(0) } — without by; must use state.value to read and write
remember { } — runs its lambda only on first composition; returns the cached value on recompositions
rememberSaveable { mutableStateOf(0) } — survives recomposition AND configuration changes (rotation)
State hoisting — move state up to the parent composable when multiple children need to share or modify it
Stateless composable — takes values and callbacks as parameters, holds no internal state
Stateful composable — owns and manages its own state internally with remember
Prefer stateless composables for reusability and testability; manage state at the highest needed level

rows.description: Observable state holder — triggers recomposition when value changes
Observable list — triggers recomposition when items are added, removed, or changed
Observable map — triggers recomposition when entries change
Converts a Flow or StateFlow into Compose State — recomposes on each emission
Like collectAsState() but lifecycle-aware — stops collecting when composable is not active
Like remember but survives configuration changes by writing to the saved instance state Bundle

sections.heading: Setting Up Compose in a Project
items: Compose requires compileSdk 34+ and minSdk 21+ in build.gradle.kts
Enable Compose in the android block: buildFeatures { compose = true }
Set composeOptions: composeOptions { kotlinCompilerExtensionVersion = "1.5.x" }
Use the Compose BOM for consistent library versions: implementation(platform("androidx.compose:compose-bom:2024.x.x"))
Add UI dependencies: implementation("androidx.compose.ui:ui"), implementation("androidx.compose.material3:material3")
Add tooling: debugImplementation("androidx.compose.ui:ui-tooling"), implementation("androidx.compose.ui:ui-tooling-preview")
Add Activity Compose: implementation("androidx.activity:activity-compose:1.9.x")
Entry point in Activity: setContent { MaterialTheme { YourRootComposable() } }

rows.description: Primary entry point — sets the root Compose content for a full Compose screen
rows.example: class MainActivity : ComponentActivity() { override fun onCreate(...) { setContent { AppTheme { NavHost(...) } } } }

rows.description: Embeds a Compose composable inside a traditional View-based layout
Create a custom View subclass that renders Compose content — for library authors
rows.example: class MyComposeView : AbstractComposeView(context) { @Composable override fun Content() { MyComposable() } }

sections.heading: @Preview — Iterating Without Running the App
items: Annotate a composable with @Preview to see it rendered in Android Studio's Design panel instantly
@Preview only works on composables that take no required parameters or have all defaults provided
Use @Preview(showBackground = true) to render on a white background instead of transparent
Use @Preview(widthDp = 360, heightDp = 640) to simulate a specific screen size
Use @Preview(uiMode = UI_MODE_NIGHT_YES) to preview in dark mode
Add multiple @Preview annotations to a single composable to see multiple configurations at once
@PreviewParameter — pass sample data to a composable preview without a ViewModel
Previews never run real code (network, database) — use hardcoded or fake data

rows.mistake: Performing side effects directly in the composable body
rows.fix: Use LaunchedEffect, SideEffect, or DisposableEffect for side effects — never launch coroutines or write to state directly in the body

rows.mistake: Holding state in a ViewModel inside a composable with remember
rows.fix: ViewModel state belongs in the ViewModel — collect it with collectAsStateWithLifecycle(); use remember only for local transient UI state

rows.mistake: Creating objects or lambdas directly inside composable body without remember
rows.fix: Use remember { } for objects that should not be recreated on every recomposition — prevents unnecessary allocations

rows.mistake: Not providing contentDescription on Image and Icon
rows.fix: Always provide contentDescription for accessibility; use null explicitly for purely decorative images

rows.mistake: Using Modifier.fillMaxSize() on a child inside a LazyColumn item
rows.fix: LazyColumn has infinite height — use Modifier.fillMaxWidth() for items, not fillMaxSize()
Compose cannot observe changes to a regular mutableListOf — use mutableStateListOf() or a SnapshotStateList for observable lists

rows.mistake: Ignoring the padding parameter passed by Scaffold
rows.fix: Always apply the PaddingValues from Scaffold's content lambda: Modifier.padding(paddingValues) — otherwise content is hidden behind system bars

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Jetpack Compose is a paradigm shift in Android UI development — from imperative XML manipulation to declarative Kotlin-only UI description. Composable functions are the atomic unit of Compose UI, the Modifier system replaces XML attributes, and state drives all UI updates automatically. Setting up Compose requires enabling it in the build configuration and using the BOM for version management. The @Preview annotation enables rapid iteration without running the app. As you progress through the following Compose topics, these fundamentals — composables, modifiers, and state — will be the foundation everything else builds upon.
